did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a Hybrid Deep Learning Framework (HDLF) for skin cancer detection. It uses Convolutional Neural Networks (specifically ResNet-50 via transfer learning) for feature extraction from dermoscopic images and integrates Machine Learning classifiers (Random Forest and Support Vector Machine) for classification. The study evaluates this AI model on dermatology datasets (ISIC and HAM10000) to detect malignant lesions like melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a 'Hybrid Deep Learning Framework (HDLF)' for skin cancer detection. It details a specific methodology involving CNNs (ResNet-50) and ML classifiers (SVM, Random Forest), describes data collection from ISIC and HAM10000 datasets, and reports new empirical results including accuracy (93.28%), AUC (0.978), and per-class metrics. Although published in a conference proceedings (WcCST-2026), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Skin Cancer Detection Using Convolutional Neural Networks and Machine Learning Algorithms
journal: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
brief_summary: This study proposes a Hybrid Deep Learning Framework (HDLF) for skin cancer detection that integrates ResNet-50 for feature extraction with Random Forest and Support Vector Machine classifiers. The model was trained and evaluated on dermoscopic images from the ISIC and HAM10000 datasets, utilizing data augmentation and normalization techniques to handle class imbalance and variability. The primary evaluation approach involved comparing the HDLF against baseline models, including end-to-end CNNs and traditional machine learning with hand-crafted features, using metrics such as accuracy, AUC, and per-class recall. The proposed framework achieved a multi-class accuracy of 93.28% and an average AUC of 0.978, significantly outperforming the simple CNN baseline of 85.12%. Additionally, the model demonstrated a high melanoma recall rate of 99.00% and low inference latency of 385 ms, indicating its potential for clinically feasible, real-time differential diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Seven dermatological lesion classes (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories. The model uses ResNet-50 for feature extraction and Random Forest/SVM classifiers for prediction. Performance is evaluated using accuracy (93.28%), AUC (0.978), precision, recall, and F1-score per class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018

use.training: Primary source of data for training the Hybrid Deep Learning Framework (HDLF).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the degree to which features are redundant from each other by analyzing the correlation of feature vectors.
To identify the most appropriate ML classifier and determine the best hyperparameter settings for that classifier on the training dataset.
To evaluate diagnostic performance and calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for each of the seven skin lesion types.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, AUC, recall, etc.) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC_and_HAM10000.notes: The proposed HDLF outperformed the Simple CNN baseline (85.12%) and the ML on handcrafted features baseline (78.45%). The model achieved a high melanoma recall rate of 99.00% and an average AUC of 0.978. Inference latency was recorded at 385 ms.

Per-Class_HDLF_Performance.metric: Per-Class Precision, Recall, Specificity, and F1-Score
Per-Class_HDLF_Performance.notes: Table III provides per-class metrics for the Hybrid Framework. Note: The Abstract reports a Melanoma recall rate of 99.00%, while Table III reports 94.1%. The text in Section IV.G mentions a melanoma recall rate of 96.47%.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Deep Learning Framework (HDLF) combining ResNet-50 for feature extraction with Random Forest and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning with pretrained layers from CNN architectures (ResNet-50, VGG-19, InceptionV3) trained on large image datasets.

architecture_details.backbone: ResNet-50 (selected as the best performing CNN architecture among ResNet-50, VGG-19, and InceptionV3 for feature extraction).
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Deep feature vectors extracted from the CNN backbone.
architecture_details.feature_selection: Pearson product-moment correlation coefficients used to evaluate feature redundancy and select features.
classifiers: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel
architecture_details.integration: CNN feature extractor combined with the selected ML classifier.

training_details.data_augmentation: Geometric transformations (rotation, flipping, scaling) and photometric transformations (brightness, color changes) to simulate realistic clinical scenarios.
training_details.normalization: Resizing to 224x224 and normalization using pixel intensity values in range [1-2] or z-score normalization.
training_details.hyperparameter_tuning: Cross-validation used to identify the most appropriate ML classifier and determine best hyperparameter settings.
training_details.optimization: Not explicitly reported for the CNN backbone; hyperparameters for ML classifiers tuned via cross-validation.

classification_or_output_head.details: Random Forest and Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel used for final classification of extracted feature vectors.

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization using pixel intensity values in range [1-2] or z-score normalization
Data augmentation including geometric transformations (rotation, flipping, scaling, zooming, randomized cropping) and photometric transformations (brightness, color changes)
Noise and artifact removal using DullRazor algorithm, Wiener filtering, and contrast enhancement to remove hairs and skin pixel damages
Class imbalance resolution via undersampling majority class, SMOTE in feature space, and augmenting minority class

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, flipping, image zooming, scaling, and randomized cropping to simulate changes in position, angle, and size.
Adjustments to brightness and color to simulate changing devices, lighting conditions, and skin color variations.
Augmenting the minority class using image augmentations to resolve class-size imbalances.
Applied in the feature space to balance class distribution.
Removing samples from the majority class to achieve a more balanced distribution.

conclusions: The proposed Hybrid Deep Learning Framework (HDLF), which integrates ResNet-50 for feature extraction with Random Forest and Support Vector Machine classifiers, achieved a multi-class accuracy of 93.28% and an average AUC of 0.978 on the ISIC and HAM10000 datasets.
The HDLF significantly outperformed baseline models, including a simple end-to-end CNN (85.12% accuracy) and traditional machine learning on hand-crafted features (78.45% accuracy).
The model demonstrated high clinical feasibility with a low inference latency of 385 ms, enabling potential for real-time differential diagnosis.
The framework showed strong generalization capabilities, mitigating overfitting through data augmentation and rigorous preprocessing, resulting in converging training and validation loss curves.
The HDLF achieved a high recall rate for melanoma (reported as 94.1% in per-class metrics and 99.00% in the abstract), minimizing false negatives for this critical malignant lesion type.

Explainable AI (XAI) using Grad-CAM heatmaps confirmed that the model relies on clinically relevant features, such as irregular borders and variable color, to make predictions, thereby enhancing transparency and trust for clinical integration.

The hybrid approach of combining deep CNN-generated features with ML classifiers provides higher classification stability and accuracy compared to CNN-only techniques, which can struggle with variability in skin lesion shape, color, size, and texture.
important_remarks: The paper is a conference proceeding (WcCST-2026) rather than a peer-reviewed journal article, which may limit the depth of methodological scrutiny and reproducibility details.

There are significant inconsistencies in reported performance metrics: the Abstract claims a Melanoma recall rate of 99.00%, while Table III reports 94.1% and Section IV.G states 96.47%; similarly, Table II reports Simple CNN accuracy as 86.28% while the Abstract and other sections cite 85.12%.

The study relies entirely on public datasets (ISIC and HAM10000) without any external clinical validation or independent test set from a different institution, limiting the assessment of generalizability to real-world clinical populations.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed HDLF and baseline models, relying solely on descriptive metrics.

The methodology section uses future tense ('will be applied', 'will be integrated'), suggesting the text may describe a proposed framework or protocol rather than a completed, fully executed experiment, raising questions about the finality of the reported results.
The dataset size target is mentioned as '20,000 or more' images, but the exact number of samples used for training, validation, and testing splits is not explicitly provided in the text or tables.

The model's clinical feasibility claim is supported by an inference latency of 385 ms, but the hardware environment used for this measurement is not specified, which is critical for assessing real-world deployment potential.

While Grad-CAM is used for explainability, the paper does not provide a quantitative or qualitative validation of whether the highlighted regions align with dermatologist consensus, relying only on visual inspection of heatmaps.
